cot_prefix: Okay, so Alice and Bob are playing this game with tokens. The rules are that they take turns removing either 1 or 4 tokens from the stack, and Alice goes first. The person who takes the last token wins. I need to figure out how many numbers n (from 1 to 2024) are such that Bob can guarantee a win no matter how Alice plays. First, I think this is a classic combinatorial game theory problem. These types of problems often involve figuring out which positions are "winning" positions and which are "losing" positions. A winning position means that the player whose turn it is can force a win no matter what the opponent does. A losing position is the opposite—the player is going to lose if the opponent plays optimally. So, for each number of tokens n, we need to determine if it's a winning or losing position. Since the players can remove 1 or 4 tokens, the idea is probably to use dynamic programming or some recursive approach. Let me try to formalize this. Let me define an array dp where dp[n] is true if the current player can force a win with n tokens remaining, and false otherwise. The base case would be dp[0] = false because if there are no tokens left, the current player can't make a move and thus loses. Wait, actually, the player who removes the last token wins. So if there are zero tokens, it's the previous player who took the last token and won. Therefore, the current player cannot make a move and loses. So yes, dp[0] = false. Now, for n ≥ 1, the
